heading: TONUS TERTIUS — Quod symbolum, et quam convenientiam habeat homo cum angelis. Caput I
card_summary: This chunk opens Tonus Tertius, Chapter I, with Zorzi asking what symbolic correspondence and harmony exists between humanity and the angels. Having already disposed and ordered the human being in the prior sections, Zorzi now surveys the shared nature of humans and angels: both possess reason, are capable of intellection, tend toward the same end, serve the same Lord, and drink from the same spiritual source — Christ — citing 1 Corinthians 10:4 and Augustine on the angels consulting the Logos as the fount of life. The analogy of a rider guiding a horse with gentle rein is introduced to capture how angels, though of different species, govern and accompany humans on their spiritual journey. Peter's remark that angels desire to look into these things (1 Peter 1:12) is cited to reinforce the shared eschatological orientation of angels and embodied souls.
essay:
## Overview

Tonus Tertius opens with a programmatic statement of purpose: having established the constitution and ordering of the human being and the degrees and gifts distributed to it, Zorzi now turns to the question of correspondences (*symbolum* and *convenientia*) between humanity and the higher created orders, above all the angels. The angels function, in Zorzi's scheme, as *individui comites* — inseparable traveling companions — for the soul's ascent, and so understanding the structural harmony between the two natures is a prerequisite for any coherent account of spiritual progress.

## Chapter I: The Fourfold Convenientia of Humans and Angels

Zorzi identifies several dimensions of shared nature between humans and angels.

**1. Rational and intellectual participation.** Both are *rationis participes* and *intelligentiae capaces*, sharing in reason and capable of intelligence. They tend toward the same final end (*in eundem finem tendant*) and are immediate servants of the same Lord (*eiusdem Domini immediati servi*). This is straightforwardly Scholastic and Neoplatonic: the rational soul and the angelic intellect occupy adjacent rungs on the hierarchy of being, differentiated in mode but unified in orientation.

**2. Incorruptibility and perpetuity.** Both are incorruptible and perpetual, but the mode differs: angels enjoy an equal and continuous succession, while humans are incorruptible only according to the better part — the soul — and in the body only through a future transformation. Here Zorzi cites Paul:

> Corruptibile hoc induet incorruptionem.

<cite>p. 2168 (1 Cor 15:53)</cite>

The resurrection formula is thus embedded structurally into the anthropological account: the body's eschatological transformation is the condition under which the human being attains the full angelic mode of perpetuity.

**3. Drinking from the same spiritual rock.** Both humans and angels draw from the same source, the spiritual rock that is Christ. Zorzi invokes the Pauline typology of the Exodus:

> Bibebant autem de spiritali consequenti eos petra, petra autem erat Christus, ipsis quidem praevisa, nobis autem sculpta, erecta, et immensa aqua completa, ut de plenitudine eius omnes plene suscipere possimus.

<cite>p. 2168 (1 Cor 10:4, amplified)</cite>

The gloss is notable: for the angels, Christ was *praevisa* — foreseen, anticipated — while for humans he was *sculpta*, *erecta*, and filled with immense water, that is, historically incarnated, embodied in a physical monument from whose fullness all may draw. This distinction between angelic pre-vision and human post-incarnational reception is a fine Christological point, situating the Incarnation as an event that transforms the terms of access to the divine source differently for each order.

**4. Augustine and 1 Peter on angelic consultation of the Logos.** Zorzi reinforces the shared spiritual drinking with Augustine's testimony:

> Ipsum consulunt angeli, tamquam vitae fontem.

<cite>p. 2168–2169 (Augustine)</cite>

And with 1 Peter 1:12 on the angels' desire to look into salvific matters (*desiderant angeli prospicere*) — not merely out of curiosity but as a continuous drawing from the same source, both for themselves and for the souls in their care while those souls remain imprisoned in the body (*hoc corporeo carcere detrusi*).

## The Rider-and-Horse Analogy

Despite the difference of species between angels and humans, Zorzi insists that the *convenientia* is no less real. He deploys a striking comparison: angels relate to humans as a rider to a horse — the rider guides with a small bit (*parvo freno*) and a light touch of the spurs (*modico calcarium tactu*), using the horse's own bodily motion to convey a kind of prudence for completing the journey. The analogy naturalizes angelic direction without eliminating human agency; the human being is not simply dragged but guided through its own proper mode of locomotion.

> Et quamvis specie dicantur differre ab hominibus, non minus tamen ipsis conveniunt, quam ii cum equis, quibus insident, et parvo freno eos deducunt ad libitum, atque modico calcarium tactu, dum corporis gestu impellunt, et instruunt, prudentiam quandam ad peragendum iter eis ingeren-

<cite>p. 2169 (text breaks mid-sentence at chunk boundary)</cite>

## Significance in the Larger Project

This chapter marks the beginning of Tonus Tertius's angelological turn. Having worked through the microcosm (the human being's internal constitution, its degrees and gifts) in Toni I and II, Zorzi now situates the human being in its relational cosmos — angels above, material creation below. The *convenientia* framework is typically Zorzianic: he uses structural analogy and harmonic correspondence as the primary explanatory tool, here mapping the human-angel relationship onto a shared metaphysical topology (same end, same source, same Lord) differentiated only by mode and species. The Christological center — both orders drink from the rock that is Christ — ensures that the angelological excursus remains firmly within Christian theological bounds, while the Augustinian citation and the Petrine allusion anchor it patristically. The chunk ends mid-sentence, indicating that the elaboration of the rider-horse analogy continues in the following chapter.
